research_question: Can contrastive learning with opponent hand identity (J/Q/K) as supervision produce a trainable representation that separates states by opponent hand?
answer: Yes. The encoder achieves 62.8% linear probe accuracy (vs. 33% chance), confirming that hand-identity supervision creates opponent-discriminative features.
key_findings: Linear probe accuracy: 62.8% vs 33.3% chance baseline (+29.5 pp)
Encoder uses 1 effective dimension (99.76% variance in PC1) — ordinal collapse onto hand rank axis
Spearman rho=0.325 (p<1e-120) confirms embedding distance correlates with hand label distance
Representation is linearly separable: a logistic regression on 8-dim embeddings decodes hand identity at 2x chance
Dimensional collapse is semantically meaningful: the 1D axis encodes opponent hand rank (J < Q < K)
